title: Musk Takes the Stand: 'If We Make It OK to Loot a Charity, Charitable Giving Will Be Destroyed'
summary: Elon Musk took the witness stand on Tuesday in a high-stakes trial that could redefine the future of artificial intelligence. The world's richest person is suing OpenAI, the maker of ChatGPT, alleging that its cofounder Sam Altman and president Greg Brockman abandoned the company's original charitable mission to build safe AI for humanity and instead turned it into a profit-seeking juggernaut. "If we make it OK to loot a charity, the entire foundation of charitable giving in America will be destroyed," Musk testified in a California courtroom. "That's my concern." Musk, who helped fund OpenAI's early days with about $38 million, claims he was betrayed when the nonprofit created a for-profit entity in 2019 — 13 months after he left its board. His lawyer, Steven Molo, told jurors that a major turning point came in January 2023 when Microsoft invested $10 billion in OpenAI. "It violated every commitment [the defendants] made, not just to Elon, but to the world," Molo said. OpenAI's lawyer, Bill Savitt, painted a different picture. He argued that Musk himself pushed for OpenAI to become a for-profit business, one he could lead as CEO. "What he cares about is Elon Musk being on top," Savitt said. "We are here because Mr. Musk didn't get his way." Savitt said OpenAI needed to create a for-profit arm to buy computing power and hire top scientists to compete with Google's DeepMind. Musk's own AI company, xAI, is now part of SpaceX. The trial has already seen drama: U.S. District Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers admonished Musk for social media posts calling Altman "Scam Altman" and urged him to "control your propensity to use social media." Musk agreed to minimize his posts. He is seeking $150 billion in damages, with proceeds going to OpenAI's charitable arm. The outcome could influence how AI companies balance profit and public good.
why_it_matters: This lawsuit could decide whether a nonprofit can legally transform into a profit-driven company after taking early donations — a question that affects not just AI but the entire charitable sector, and it will shape how future advanced technologies are developed and who gets to control them.

keywords.explanation: Claiming that someone did something wrong, usually before it is proven in court — here, Musk claims OpenAI broke its promises.
One of the people who started a company or organization right from the beginning.
A huge, powerful force or organization that is hard to stop — used here to describe OpenAI turning into a big profit-driven company.
To steal goods or money, especially during chaos; Musk used it to say turning a charity into a profit company is like robbing a charity.
To warn or tell someone off firmly, as the judge did to Musk about his social media posts.
Money that a court orders one side to pay the other as compensation for a wrong done.

questions.question: What is Elon Musk's main claim against OpenAI and its leaders?
options: They abandoned the original charitable mission to build safe AI for humanity
They refused to hire him as CEO
questions.correct_answer: They abandoned the original charitable mission to build safe AI for humanity

questions.question: According to Musk's lawyer, what was a major turning point that violated commitments?
How did OpenAI's lawyer describe Musk's motivation?
options: He cares about being on top and didn't get his way
He wanted to shut down ChatGPT
He was trying to recruit Sam Altman
questions.correct_answer: He cares about being on top and didn't get his way

questions.question: What did the judge tell Musk to do about his social media?
options: Control his propensity to use social media and minimize posts
questions.correct_answer: Control his propensity to use social media and minimize posts

questions.question: How much money in damages is Musk seeking?
According to OpenAI, why did the company need to create a for-profit arm?
options: To pay Musk back his investment
To buy computing power and hire top scientists to compete with DeepMind
questions.correct_answer: To buy computing power and hire top scientists to compete with DeepMind

background_read: OpenAI was founded in 2015 as a nonprofit research lab with a mission to develop artificial intelligence that benefits all humanity, avoiding any single company's control. Elon Musk (the billionaire behind Tesla and SpaceX) was an early donor, giving about $38 million.

Sam Altman and Greg Brockman (cofounders and now CEO and president of OpenAI) pushed to create a for-profit arm in 2019 to raise the huge amounts of money needed to train advanced AI models — a move Musk now says was a betrayal.

Microsoft's $10 billion investment in 2023 gave OpenAI access to enormous cloud computing power, which helped launch ChatGPT to over 100 million users within months, turning OpenAI into one of the most valuable tech ventures.

Google's DeepMind is another top AI lab, originally bought by Google in 2014, showing how tech giants compete fiercely to lead in artificial general intelligence — and why OpenAI felt it needed for-profit backing.
Musk now runs his own AI company, xAI, so the lawsuit is also part of a growing fight over who will shape the future of AI and whether profit or safety should come first.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Musk testifies, warning that allowing a charity to be ‘looted’ would destroy charitable giving in America.
└─ specific: 'If we make it OK to loot a charity…'
KEY EVENT: The lawsuit itself — Musk sues OpenAI and its cofounders for abandoning the nonprofit mission.
├─ ACCUSATION: OpenAI turned into a profit-seeking juggernaut after Microsoft’s $10B investment
└─ DEFENSE: OpenAI says Musk pushed for a for-profit structure and wanted to lead it as CEO
├─ MUSK’S SIDE: He was betrayed; his $38M early funding was misused
└─ OPENAI’S SIDE: Musk didn’t get control and now is suing out of spite
EVIDENCE: Courtroom details — the judge’s admonishment about Musk’s social media, the $150B damages claim
OPEN QUESTION: Will the trial set a legal line for how AI nonprofits can shift to for-profit, and who gets to steer powerful AI?

perspectives.description: Musk’s position is that a charity's donors and the public trust are sacred; if a nonprofit simply takes the money and later chases profit, donors everywhere might lose confidence. This view prioritizes the founding promise over financial survival, and sees the for-profit pivot as a betrayal of humanity's safety.

perspectives.perspective: Practical survival in a costly field
perspectives.description: OpenAI argues that building truly safe, cutting-edge AI requires billions of dollars — more than any charity could raise. By creating a for-profit arm, they attracted the investment needed to stay competitive with giants like Google and actually build real-world AI services, rather than staying a small idealistic lab.

A more neutral observation is that this trial is about control as much as mission. Musk wanted to run OpenAI himself, and when the board refused, he left. Now, as the head of a rival AI company, his lawsuit may reflect a personal vendetta, highlighting how ego and ambition can drive even the biggest tech disputes.

perspectives.perspective: What this means for AI governance
perspectives.description: Regardless of who wins, the case could pressure lawmakers to create clearer rules for how AI companies are structured. If courts can't easily decide, new legislation might require that AI labs with a public-safety promise cannot switch to for-profit without strict oversight, influencing startups and investments for years.
